questions.questionText: A child with esotropia (convergent squint) that is fully corrected by wearing hypermetropic spectacles has:
questions.optionB: Accommodative esotropia (convergent squint caused by excessive accommodative effort to compensate for hypermetropia → excessive convergence → treat with hypermetropic correction)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Accommodative esotropia = hypermetropia → excess accommodation → excess convergence → corrected by plus glasses. Why correct: Synkinetic relationship: accommodation → convergence. In hypermetropia, extra accommodation to focus → excessive convergence → esotropia. Correction: full hypermetropic prescription. If fully corrected → purely accommodative. Why A wrong: Non-accommodative (infantile) — present <6 months, large constant angle, needs surgery. Why C wrong: Exotropia (divergent): intermittent initially, may need surgery. Why D wrong: Paralytic (incomitant): angle varies in different gaze directions, diplopia, nerve palsy. Clinical relevance: Cover-uncover test: detects tropia (manifest squint). Cover test: uncovers latent deviation (phoria). Hirschberg test: corneal light reflex position estimates deviation. Exam trap: Accommodative = fully corrected by glasses → NO surgery. Non-accommodative or partially accommodative → surgery needed. Memory aid: 'Accommodative eso = Accommodation drives convergence = specs fix it.' Reference: Khurana – Strabismus.
